subsystem: Poppler PDF library — embedded-file (file attachment) extraction. This is EmbFile::save2, a method of the EmbFile class that streams the decoded bytes of an embedded file stream object out to a caller-supplied stdio FILE*. It is the low-level copy routine behind the public 'save this attachment to disk' path for PDF /EmbeddedFile / /FileSpec objects.
provenance: Identified by the member/API names rather than an SPDX tag (none present). The class name EmbFile and the member m_objStr, together with the Object-stream API isStream()/streamReset()/streamGetChar() and the unlikely() branch-hint macro, are the exact vocabulary of Poppler's Object class (poppler/Object.h) and its FileSpec/EmbFile code (poppler/FileSpec.cc). The header banner names the file corpus/train/train_0089_cwe670.cpp and the leading comment tags it 'primevul_train_3452_fixed', indicating a PrimeVul-derived sample presented as the post-fix revision.
trust_boundary: The bytes copied here originate inside a PDF document — an untrusted, attacker-supplied file format. m_objStr is a stream Object taken from the PDF's embedded-file dictionary; its decoded content (through whatever /Filter chain the stream declares: FlateDecode, ASCII85, etc.) is fully attacker-controlled in both length and value. The other side of the boundary is the FILE* f, an OS file handle owned by the caller. Untrusted surface: the number of bytes the stream yields (unbounded), the values of those bytes, and whether the stream decoder itself reports a mid-stream error. The FILE* and its validity are a caller contract, not attacker-controlled, but must still be defended.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the method save2 on the EmbFile class. It takes a single parameter f, an already-opened stdio stream (FILE*), and returns a bool that signals success/failure to the caller. The name 'save2' and the boolean return are the Poppler convention for the internal worker that the public EmbFile::save(...) wraps after it has opened the destination path. The method reads no explicit source argument because the source is the object's own member m_objStr — the embedded file's stream Object.

Declares the loop variable that will hold each byte read from the stream. Critically it is declared as int, not char. This is the correct type for holding the result of a getchar-style API: it must be able to represent every possible byte value (0-255) AND the distinct out-of-band sentinel EOF (which is negative, typically -1). Using int here is what makes the EOF test on line 9 sound; a char would conflate a legitimate 0xFF data byte with EOF on platforms where char is signed.

A precondition gate. It asks the source Object m_objStr whether it actually is a stream; if it is not, the function bails out early returning false without touching f. The unlikely() macro is a compiler branch hint (built on __builtin_expect) telling the optimizer this early-exit path is rarely taken. This guard defends the subsequent streamReset()/streamGetChar() calls, which are only valid on Object instances whose type is objStream; calling them on a non-stream Object would be a type-confusion / undefined operation. This is the type-safety invariant for the rest of the body.

Rewinds/initializes the stream for reading from its beginning. In Poppler, streamReset() re-seeks the underlying Stream and, importantly, (re)builds the filter/decoder chain (e.g. it instantiates the FlateStream and calls reset on it) so that subsequent streamGetChar() calls return decoded bytes. This must be called before the read loop; without it the decode state is undefined. It has a void return, so there is no success/failure signal available at this call site.

The copy loop. Each iteration calls streamGetChar() on the source, which decodes and returns the next byte as an int in 0..255, or EOF (-1) when the stream is exhausted. The assignment-in-condition stores the value into c and then compares against EOF; the loop continues until EOF is seen. For every non-EOF byte it calls fputc(c, f) to append that byte to the destination FILE*. This is a straight byte-for-byte streaming copy with no fixed-size intermediate buffer — the loop bound is entirely determined by when the (attacker-controlled) stream reports EOF.

After the loop terminates (streamGetChar returned EOF), the function unconditionally returns true to report success. There is no flush, no fflush/fsync, and no inspection of the stream error state or the destination stream error state before declaring success — the truth of 'true' rests entirely on the assumption that reaching EOF means the whole file was copied intact.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The return value of fputc is never checked, yet the function unconditionally returns true on line 12. fputc returns EOF on a write error — a full disk (ENOSPC), a quota violation, a broken pipe, or an I/O error on the destination. When that happens the loop keeps calling fputc on an already-errored stream and then reports success, so the caller believes it wrote a complete, faithful copy of the attachment when the on-disk file is silently truncated or corrupted. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: propagate write failure. A correct version checks 'if (fputc(c, f) == EOF) return false;' (or checks ferror(f) before returning), so that a failed extraction is reported rather than masked. Impact is data-integrity/silent-corruption rather than memory safety.

The FILE *f parameter is dereferenced (via fputc(c, f)) on line 10 with no NULL check. If a caller reaches save2 with f == NULL — for example because an upstream fopen() of the user-chosen output path failed and its result was not validated before calling this worker — then fputc(c, NULL) is undefined behavior and will crash the process (NULL/invalid FILE* dereference inside libc). This is a latent defect: the bug lives in the fopen-then-call caller contract, but this signature offers no defense. The invariant is that f must be a valid, writable stdio handle; the routine should assert or early-return on a NULL f.

The loop treats EOF as the sole, benign stop condition and cannot distinguish clean end-of-stream from a mid-stream decode error. Poppler stream decoders (FlateStream, etc.) signal a decode failure on malformed/truncated compressed data by returning EOF early — the same sentinel used for normal end. Because a malformed embedded stream in an attacker's PDF can make streamGetChar() return EOF prematurely, save2 will stop, write only the partial decoded prefix, and still return true. The result is a truncated attachment silently presented as a complete, valid extraction. The invariant is that the copy must confirm the stream reached genuine end and did not error; without a stream error query, integrity of the extracted file is not guaranteed against hostile input.

streamReset() has no return value, so any failure to (re)build the decode chain — e.g. an unsupported or corrupt /Filter in the attacker's stream dictionary — is invisible here. The following read loop then either yields nothing or immediately hits EOF, and the function again returns true, producing a zero-byte 'successful' extraction. Additionally, if m_objStr is shared state that another thread or a re-entrant call could reset concurrently, the read position is not owned exclusively by this loop; Poppler's Object stream cursor is not designed for concurrent readers. The invariant is that the reset must have succeeded and the stream cursor must be exclusively owned for the duration of the copy.

summary: A small, memory-safe streaming copy of an embedded PDF file's decoded stream to a FILE*. There is no buffer, length field, or pointer arithmetic here, so classic overflow/underflow classes do not apply. The real, genuine defects are integrity/robustness ones: the unchecked fputc return value and the EOF-only stop condition let a write failure or a malformed/truncated attacker stream produce a silently truncated or corrupt output file while the function still returns true, and the FILE* parameter is dereferenced with no NULL guard. Overall risk is low (no memory-corruption primitive); findings are low-severity data-integrity and robustness issues in the implied implementation contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: bool EmbFile::save2(FILE *f) — method signature / FILE* parameter
reviewed_anchors.reason: f is dereferenced without a NULL check; latent NULL-FILE* deref if caller's fopen failed.
Correctly declared int (not char), so it can hold all 0..255 byte values plus EOF; the EOF comparison on line 9 is therefore sound. No defect.
Correctly guards the stream API calls against a non-stream Object (type-confusion defense); this is the safe, present precondition, not a bug.
void return hides reset/filter-build failure and offers no exclusive-ownership guarantee on the shared stream cursor; can yield a silent empty/partial copy.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: while ((c = m_objStr.streamGetChar()) != EOF) — read loop / stop condition
reviewed_anchors.reason: EOF is used as the only terminator and cannot be distinguished from a mid-stream decode error on malformed input, allowing silent truncation reported as success.
Return value ignored; write errors (ENOSPC/I/O) are not propagated and the function still returns true — silent output corruption/truncation.
Success is returned without inspecting write errors or stream error state; this is the sink that converts the read/write-error gaps above into a false success signal. Grouped with the fputc finding.

findings.title: Unchecked fputc return value — write failures masked, function returns true on corrupt/truncated output
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value / improper error handling (silent data corruption)
findings.invariant: Every write to the destination must be checked; a fputc returning EOF (write error such as ENOSPC, quota, EIO, EPIPE) must abort the copy and cause save2 to return false. Equivalently, ferror(f) must be consulted before returning true.
findings.failure_mode: On a full disk or any I/O error mid-copy, fputc returns EOF, the loop keeps writing to the errored stream, and the method returns true. The caller believes the embedded file was extracted intact while the on-disk copy is truncated or corrupt — a silent integrity failure for what is often a security-relevant 'save this attachment' operation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the implementation checks fputc's return (or ferror(f)) and returns false on the first write error instead of unconditionally returning true.

findings.title: EOF cannot be distinguished from a mid-stream decode error — malformed embedded stream yields silent truncation reported as success
findings.bug_class: Unchecked/ambiguous error condition on untrusted input (improper handling of exceptional conditions)
findings.invariant: The copy must confirm the source stream reached a genuine end-of-data and did not abort on a decode error; a premature EOF from a corrupt /Filter chain must not be treated as successful completion.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker crafts a PDF whose embedded-file stream has a truncated or malformed compressed body. The FlateStream (or other decoder) returns EOF early; the loop stops, only the partial decoded prefix is written, and save2 returns true. The user receives a truncated file believed to be a faithful extraction.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether Poppler's streamGetChar exposes a distinct error/error-flag query, and whether the implementation checks it before treating EOF as clean completion.

findings.title: FILE* parameter dereferenced without NULL check — latent crash if caller passes a failed fopen result
findings.invariant: f must be a valid, writable stdio handle; the routine should reject a NULL f (early return false or assert) before performing any fputc.
findings.failure_mode: If a caller opens the output path with fopen() and does not validate the result before invoking save2, f is NULL and fputc(c, NULL) on line 10 is undefined behavior, crashing the process (denial of service). This is a latent defect in the caller contract that this signature does not defend against.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether every caller validates the fopen result before calling save2, or whether save2 itself guards against a NULL f.

findings.title: streamReset() failure is invisible (void return) — unsupported/corrupt filter can yield a silent zero-byte 'successful' extraction
findings.bug_class: Ignored initialization failure / improper handling of exceptional conditions
findings.invariant: The stream must be successfully reset and its decode chain built before the read loop, and the stream cursor must be exclusively owned for the copy's duration; failure to (re)initialize must not be reported as success.
findings.failure_mode: If m_objStr carries an unsupported or corrupt /Filter, streamReset() cannot be observed to fail (void return); the subsequent loop immediately sees EOF and save2 returns true, producing a zero-byte output presented as a complete extraction. If m_objStr's cursor is shared/re-entrantly reset, the read position is not owned exclusively by this loop.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the implementation verifies the stream/decoder initialized correctly and holds exclusive access to m_objStr's read cursor across the loop.

audit_checklist: Confirm fputc's return (or ferror(f)) is checked and that any write error aborts the loop and makes save2 return false instead of true.
Confirm the loop distinguishes a clean end-of-stream from a mid-stream decode error (query the stream/decoder error state) before declaring success on attacker-supplied embedded streams.
Confirm every caller validates the fopen()/destination FILE* before invoking save2, or add a NULL-f guard in save2 itself.
Confirm streamReset() success is verifiable (or that failure is otherwise detected) so an unsupported/corrupt filter does not produce a silent zero-byte 'successful' extraction.
Confirm exclusive ownership of m_objStr's stream cursor for the duration of the copy (no concurrent/re-entrant streamReset/streamGetChar on the same Object).
Confirm the output stream is flushed/committed (fflush/error check) and the isStream() type gate remains the sole precondition needed before streamReset()/streamGetChar().
